1.title: Climbing Stairs
1.problem_statement: You are climbing a staircase. It takes n steps to reach the top. Each time you can either climb 1 or 2 steps. How many distinct ways can you climb to the top? Return the number of distinct ways modulo 1000000007.

2.title: Check if Sorted
2.problem_statement: Given an array of integers, determine if the array is sorted in non-decreasing order. Return 1 if it is sorted, and 0 otherwise.

3.title: Check if Array is Sorted
3.problem_statement: Given an array of integers, determine if the array is sorted in non-decreasing order. Return 1 if it is sorted, otherwise return 0.

4.title: Palindrome Check
4.problem_statement: Given a string, return 1 if the string is a palindrome (reads the same forwards and backward), and 0 otherwise. Ignore spaces and case.
test_cases: assert palindrome_check('A man a plan a canal Panama') == 1

5.title: Contains Duplicate
5.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, return 1 if any value appears at least twice in the array, and return 0 if every element is distinct.

6.title: Search in Sorted Array
6.problem_statement: Given a sorted array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return 1 if the target exists in the array, otherwise return 0.

7.title: Check if Element Exists
7.problem_statement: Given a sorted array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return 1 if the `target` exists in the array, otherwise return 0.

8.title: Lemonade Change
8.problem_statement: At a lemonade stand, each lemonade costs $5. Customers are standing in a queue to buy from you, and order one at a time (in the order specified by bills). Each customer will only buy one lemonade and pay with either a $5, $10, or $20 bill. You must provide the correct change to each customer so that they can buy a lemonade. Initially, you have no money. You are given an integer array bills where bills[i] is the bill the ith customer pays. Return 1 if you can provide every customer with the correct change, or 0 if you cannot.

9.title: Non-overlapping Intervals
9.problem_statement: Given a collection of intervals, find the minimum number of intervals you need to remove to make the rest of the intervals non-overlapping. An interval [a, b] overlaps with [c, d] if a < d and c < b.

10.title: Find Element in Sorted Array
10.problem_statement: Given a sorted array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return 1 if the target exists in the array, otherwise return 0.

11.title: Find First Occurrence
11.problem_statement: Given a sorted array of integers `nums` and a target integer `target`, return the index of the first occurrence of the `target` in the array. If the `target` is not found, return -1.

12.title: Find Target in Sorted Array
12.problem_statement: Given a sorted array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, determine if the target exists in the array. Return 1 if the target exists, and 0 otherwise. You must write an algorithm with O(log n) runtime complexity.

13.title: Is Power of Three
13.problem_statement: Given an integer n, return 1 if it is a power of three. Otherwise, return 0. An integer n is a power of three, if there exists an integer x such that n == 3^x.

14.title: Maximum Sum Subarray of Size K
14.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums and a positive integer k, find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray of size k.

15.title: Palindrome Check (Recursive)
15.problem_statement: Given a string, determine if it is a palindrome using recursion. Return 1 if it is a palindrome, and 0 otherwise. Ignore case and non-alphanumeric characters.

16.title: Is Palindrome (Recursive)
16.problem_statement: Given a string, determine if it is a palindrome using recursion. Return 1 if it is a palindrome, and 0 otherwise. Ignore spaces and case.

17.title: Validate Email
17.problem_statement: Given a string, return 1 if the string is a valid email address, otherwise return 0. A valid email address follows the format: username@domain.extension, where username and domain contain only alphanumeric characters, underscores, periods, or hyphens. The extension contains only alphanumeric characters and must be between 2 and 3 characters long.
17.solution:
def validate_email(s):
    import re
    pattern = r'^[a-zA-Z0-9_.\-]+@[a-zA-Z0-9]+\.[a-zA-Z]{2,3}$'
    return 1 if re.match(pattern, s) else 0

18.title: Max Sum Subarray of Size K
18.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer k, find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray of size k. Return the maximum sum.

19.title: Palindrome Checker (Recursive)
19.problem_statement: Given a string, determine if it is a palindrome using recursion. A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backward. Return 1 if the string is a palindrome, and 0 otherwise. Ignore case and non-alphanumeric characters.
test_cases: assert palindrome_checker_recursive('A man a plan a canal Panama') == 1

20.title: Subset Sum
20.problem_statement: Given a set of non-negative integers, and a value sum, determine if there is a subset of the given set with sum equal to given sum. Return 1 if such a subset exists, otherwise return 0.

21.title: Is Power of Four
21.problem_statement: Given an integer n, return 1 if it is a power of four. Otherwise, return 0.

22.title: Valid Parentheses
22.problem_statement:
Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.

An input string is valid if:

1. Open brackets must be closed by the same type of brackets.
2. Open brackets must be closed in the correct order.
3. Every close bracket has a corresponding open bracket of the same type.

Return 1 if the string is valid, and 0 otherwise.

23.title: Palindrome Number
23.problem_statement: Given an integer x, return 1 if x is a palindrome, and 0 otherwise.

24.title: Array Contains Duplicate
24.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, return 1 if any value appears at least twice in the array, and return 0 if every element is distinct.

25.title: Is Power of Two
25.problem_statement: Given an integer n, return 1 if it is a power of two. Otherwise, return 0. An integer n is a power of two, if there exists an integer x such that n == 2^x.

26.title: Check if a number is a power of 2
26.problem_statement: Given an integer n, determine if it is a power of two. Return 1 if it is, and 0 otherwise.

27.title: Kth Largest Element
27.problem_statement: Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `k`, return 1 if the kth largest element is greater than 0, otherwise return 0. Assume k is always valid: 1 <= k <= array's length.

28.title: Check for Duplicates
28.problem_statement: Given an array of integers, determine if the array contains any duplicates. Return 1 if any value appears at least twice in the array, and return 0 if every element is distinct.

29.title: Simple Email Validation
29.problem_statement:
Given a string, determine if it is a valid email address based on a simplified regular expression. The email should have the format 'username@domain.extension', where:

*   username: can contain letters (a-z, A-Z), numbers (0-9), underscores (_), periods (.), and hyphens (-).
*   domain: can contain letters (a-z, A-Z) and numbers (0-9).
*   extension: can contain letters (a-z, A-Z) and must be 2 or 3 characters long.

Return 1 if the email is valid, and 0 otherwise.
29.solution:
def simple_email_validation(s):
    import re
    pattern = r'^[a-zA-Z0-9_.\-]+@[a-zA-Z0-9]+\.[a-zA-Z]{2,3}$'
    return 1 if re.match(pattern, s) else 0

30.title: Check if Number is a Power of Two
30.problem_statement: Given an integer n, return 1 if it is a power of two. Otherwise, return 0.

31.title: Range Sum Query
31.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums, determine if the sum of elements from index 0 to index i (inclusive) is greater than or equal to a target value k for all i from 0 to n-1. Return 1 if true, otherwise return 0.

32.title: Find Target
32.problem_statement: Given a sorted array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return 1 if the target exists in the array, otherwise return 0.

33.title: Prefix Check
33.problem_statement: Given a list of strings `words` and a string `prefix`, determine if any word in `words` starts with the given `prefix`. Return 1 if a word starts with the prefix, otherwise return 0.

34.title: Target Sum with Plus or Minus
34.problem_statement: Given an array of integers `nums` and a target integer `target`, determine if it is possible to reach the target sum by performing either addition or subtraction on each number in `nums`. You must use each number exactly once. Return 1 if it is possible to reach the target sum, otherwise return 0.

35.title: Valid Email
35.problem_statement: Given a string, return 1 if the string is a valid simplified email address, otherwise return 0. A valid simplified email address must contain exactly one '@' symbol, at least one '.' after the '@' symbol, and only consist of alphanumeric characters, '.', '@', '+', '-', '_'.

36.title: Subarray Sum
36.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer k, return 1 if there exists a contiguous subarray of size k that sums up to a target value, otherwise return 0.

37.title: Check Subset
37.problem_statement: Given two arrays of integers, arr1 and arr2, return 1 if arr1 is a subset of arr2 (meaning all elements in arr1 are also present in arr2), otherwise return 0.

38.title: Palindrome Check using Stack and Queue
38.problem_statement: Given a string s, return 1 if it is a palindrome, returning 0 otherwise. Solve this problem by using a stack and a queue. A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backward.

39.title: Power of Two Check (Bit Manipulation)
39.problem_statement: Given a positive integer n, return 1 if n is a power of two, otherwise return 0. Solve it using bit manipulation.

40.title: Subarray Sum Greater Than Target

41.title: Power of Two
41.problem_statement: Given an integer n, return 1 if n is a power of two, otherwise return 0.

42.title: Check Sorted Array
42.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums, return 1 if the array is sorted in ascending order, otherwise return 0.

43.title: Maximum Items
43.problem_statement: You are given a list of item prices and a budget. You want to buy as many items as possible. Return the maximum number of items you can buy within your budget. You should use a greedy approach to solve this problem.

44.title: Unique Characters in String
44.problem_statement: Given a string, return 1 if all characters in the string are unique, otherwise return 0.

45.title: Balanced Parentheses
45.problem_statement:
Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid. An input string is valid if:
1. Open brackets must be closed by the same type of brackets.
2. Open brackets must be closed in the correct order.

46.title: StartsWith
46.problem_statement: Given two strings, text and prefix, return 1 if the string text starts with the string prefix, otherwise return 0.
46.solution:
def starts_with(text, prefix):
    return 1 if text.startswith(prefix) else 0

47.title: Target Sum Subset Exists
47.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, determine if there exists a subset of nums that sums up to the target. Return 1 if such a subset exists, and 0 otherwise.

48.title: Pair Sum in Sorted Array
48.problem_statement: Given a sorted array of integers nums and an integer target, return 1 if there exists two numbers in the array that add up to the target, otherwise return 0.

49.title: String Starts and Ends with Pattern
49.problem_statement: Given a string, check if it starts with the character 'a' and ends with the character 'b'. Return 1 if it does, and 0 otherwise.
49.solution:

    return 1 if s.startswith('a') and s.endswith('b') else 0

50.title: Recursive Array Search

52.title: Smallest Element Check
52.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return 1 if the smallest element in the array is greater than or equal to the target, otherwise return 0.
52.solution:

53.title: String Starts With

54.title: Array Contains Element (Recursive)
54.problem_statement: Given an array of integers `nums` and a target integer `target`, return 1 if the target integer is present in the array, otherwise return 0. Solve this problem using recursion.

55.title: Find Maximum
55.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums, find the maximum element using a divide and conquer approach. Return the maximum element.

56.title: String Starts and Ends With
56.problem_statement: Given a string, a prefix, and a suffix, determine if the string starts with the given prefix and ends with the given suffix. Return 1 if it does, and 0 otherwise.
56.solution:

57.title: Vowel Count
57.problem_statement: Given a string, return the number of vowels in the string. Vowels are defined as 'a', 'e', 'i', 'o', 'u' (case-insensitive).

58.title: Subarray with Target Sum (Sliding Window)
58.problem_statement: Given an array of positive integers nums and a target integer target, return 1 if there exists a contiguous subarray within nums that sums up to target. Otherwise, return 0.

59.title: Smallest Element Threshold
59.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer threshold, return 1 if the smallest element in the array is strictly less than the threshold, otherwise return 0.
59.solution:
def smallest_element_threshold(nums, threshold):
    return 1 if min(nums) < threshold else 0

60.title: Reach the End
60.problem_statement: You are given a 0-indexed array of non-negative integers `jumps`. You are initially at index 0. Each element `jumps[i]` represents the maximum jump length from index `i`. Determine if you can reach the last index of the array. Return 1 if you can reach the last index, and 0 otherwise.

65.title: Range Existence Query

Then, compare characters popped from the stack and dequeued from the queue. If they are all the same, return 1 if the string is a palindrome, otherwise return 0.

69.title: Range Maximum Check
69.problem_statement: Given an array of integers `nums`, a start index `start_index`, an end index `end_index`, and a threshold value `threshold`, return 1 if the maximum value in the subarray from `start_index` to `end_index` (inclusive) is greater than or equal to the `threshold`, otherwise return 0.
69.solution:
def range_maximum_check(nums, start_index, end_index, threshold):
    return 1 if max(nums[start_index:end_index+1]) >= threshold else 0

75.title: K-th Smallest Element Check
75.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums, an integer k, and an integer target, return 1 if the k-th smallest element in the array is less than or equal to target, otherwise return 0.

76.title: Check for Duplicates in Sorted Array
76.problem_statement: Given a sorted array of integers, return 1 if there are duplicate numbers in the array, otherwise return 0.

77.title: Jump Game Lite
77.problem_statement: Given an array of non-negative integers `nums`, where each element `nums[i]` represents the maximum jump length from index `i`, determine if it is possible to reach the last index starting from the first index (index 0). Return 1 if it is possible, and 0 otherwise.

78.title: Task Scheduling
78.problem_statement: Given a list of tasks, where each task has a duration, and a total time limit. Determine if it is possible to complete at least half of the tasks within the given time limit using a greedy approach. The greedy approach is to always choose the shortest task first. Return 1 if it is possible to complete at least half of the tasks, otherwise return 0.

79.title: Substring Check
79.problem_statement: Given two strings, `text` and `substring`, determine if `substring` is present within `text`. Return 1 if `substring` is found in `text`, and 0 otherwise.

    return 1 if substring in text else 0

80.title: Unique Elements
80.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums, return 1 if all elements in the array are unique, otherwise return 0.

81.title: Palindrome Array Check
81.problem_statement: Given an array of integers, return 1 if the array is a palindrome, otherwise return 0. An array is a palindrome if it reads the same forwards and backward.

82.title: Can Sum
82.problem_statement: Given an array of non-negative integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return 1 if there exists a subset of `nums` that sums up to `target`, otherwise return 0.

83.title: Character in String
83.problem_statement: Given a string and a character, return 1 if the character is present in the string, otherwise return 0.
83.solution:
def character_in_string(s, char):
    return 1 if char in s else 0

84.title: Unique Numbers
84.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums, return 1 if all numbers in the array are unique, otherwise return 0.

85.title: Check if Array is Sorted (Divide and Conquer)
85.problem_statement: Given an array of integers, determine if it is sorted in ascending order using a divide and conquer approach. Return 1 if the array is sorted, otherwise return 0.

86.title: Get Non-Common Strings
86.problem_statement: Write a Python function called get_non_common_strings(). The function takes in two lists of strings, str_list1 and str_list2. The function returns a list of strings that can be found in either str_list1 or str_list2, BUT NOT in both. For example, if str_list1 is ["a", "b", "c", "d"], str_list2 is ["b", "d", "e", "f"], then this function returns ["a", "c", "e", "f"].

87.title: Count Character Frequency
87.problem_statement: Write a Python function called count_char_frequency(). The function takes a single string s and returns a dictionary where each key is a character from the string, and its value is the number of times it appears. For example, count_char_frequency('hello') returns {'h': 1, 'e': 1, 'l': 2, 'o': 1}. If the string is empty, return an empty dictionary.

88.title: Group Strings By Length
88.problem_statement: Write a Python function called group_strings_by_length(). The function takes a list of strings str_list and returns a dictionary where each key is an integer representing a string length, and its value is a list of all strings from str_list with that length. For example, group_strings_by_length(['hi', 'bye', 'ok', 'hey']) returns {2: ['hi', 'ok'], 3: ['bye', 'hey']}. If the input list is empty, return an empty dictionary.

89.title: Flatten One-Level Nested List
89.problem_statement: Write a Python function called flatten_nested_list(). The function takes a list called nested where each element is either an integer or a list of integers. The function returns a single flat list containing all the integers in order. For example, flatten_nested_list([[1, 2], [3, 4], [5]]) returns [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], and flatten_nested_list([1, [2, 3], 4]) returns [1, 2, 3, 4].

90.title: Find Second Largest
90.problem_statement: Write a Python function called find_second_largest(). The function takes a list of integers nums (with at least two distinct values) and returns the second largest unique value. For example, find_second_largest([3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]) returns 6. You should NOT use Python's built-in sort() or sorted() functions.

91.title: Merge and Sort Two Lists
91.problem_statement: Write a Python function called merge_and_sort(). The function takes two lists of integers, list1 and list2, and returns a single sorted list containing all elements from both lists in non-decreasing order. You should NOT use Python's built-in sort() or sorted() functions. For example, merge_and_sort([3, 1], [4, 2]) returns [1, 2, 3, 4].

92.title: Reverse Words In Sentence
92.problem_statement: Write a Python function called reverse_words_in_sentence(). The function takes a string sentence and returns a new string where the words are in reversed order. Words are separated by single spaces. For example, reverse_words_in_sentence('hello world') returns 'world hello', and reverse_words_in_sentence('a b c') returns 'c b a'. You should NOT use Python's built-in reverse() function.
test_cases: assert reverse_words_in_sentence('a b c') == 'c b a'

93.title: Count Words In Sentence
93.problem_statement: Write a Python function called count_words_in_sentence(). The function takes a string sentence and returns the number of words in it. Words are separated by one or more whitespace characters. If the sentence is empty or contains only spaces, return 0. For example, count_words_in_sentence('hello world') returns 2, and count_words_in_sentence('') returns 0.

94.title: Matrix Transpose
94.problem_statement: Write a Python function called matrix_transpose(). The function takes a 2D list (matrix) of integers and returns its transpose. The transpose of a matrix is obtained by swapping its rows and columns. For example, matrix_transpose([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]]) returns [[1, 4], [2, 5], [3, 6]]. If the input is an empty list, return an empty list.

95.title: Get Nearest Driver
95.problem_statement: Write a Python function called get_nearest_driver() that takes in two parameters: a tuple containing the passenger's location in the form of (latitude, longitude), and a dictionary containing each driver's name and his/her current location in the form of a tuple (latitude, longitude). The function returns the name of the nearest driver to the passenger. The distance is computed using the Euclidean distance formula. For example, get_nearest_driver((0, 0), {'Alice': (1, 1), 'Bob': (5, 5)}) returns 'Alice'.
95.solution:
import math
def get_nearest_driver(passenger_location, drivers):
    nearest = None
    min_dist = float('inf')
    plat, plng = passenger_location
    for name, (dlat, dlng) in drivers.items():
        dist = math.sqrt((plat - dlat)**2 + (plng - dlng)**2)
        if dist < min_dist:
            min_dist = dist
            nearest = name
    return nearest

96.title: Word Frequency In Paragraph
96.problem_statement: Write a Python function called word_frequency_in_paragraph(). The function takes a string paragraph and returns a dictionary where each key is a word (lowercased, stripped of non-alphabetic characters) and the value is the number of times it appears. Words are separated by spaces. If the paragraph is empty, return an empty dictionary. For example, word_frequency_in_paragraph('Go go GO') returns {'go': 3}.

97.title: Table Allocation
97.problem_statement: Write a Python function called allocate_tables(). The function takes a list of tuples groups (where each tuple is (group_name, group_size)) and a dictionary tables (where each key is a table ID and value is its seating capacity). Assign each group to the smallest available table that can fit the group. Return a dictionary mapping each group_name to a table ID. If no suitable table exists for a group, skip that group. For example, allocate_tables([('A', 2), ('B', 4)], {'T1': 2, 'T2': 4, 'T3': 6}) returns {'A': 'T1', 'B': 'T2'}.

98.title: Shipping Cost Calculator
98.problem_statement: Write a Python function called calculate_shipping_cost(). The function takes a list of tuples orders, where each tuple is (item_name, weight_kg). It returns a dictionary mapping each item_name to its shipping cost. The shipping cost is calculated as follows: weight <= 1 kg costs $5.00, weight <= 5 kg costs $10.00, weight <= 20 kg costs $20.00, and weight > 20 kg costs $50.00. For example, calculate_shipping_cost([('book', 0.5), ('tv', 15)]) returns {'book': 5.0, 'tv': 20.0}.

99.title: Election Vote Counter
99.problem_statement: Write a Python function called count_votes(). The function takes a list of strings votes, where each string is a candidate's name. The function returns a dictionary mapping each candidate's name to the number of votes they received. Additionally, it should return the name of the winner (candidate with the most votes). Return a tuple (vote_dict, winner). If there are no votes, return ({}, None). For example, count_votes(['Alice', 'Bob', 'Alice']) returns ({'Alice': 2, 'Bob': 1}, 'Alice').

100.title: Encode Caesar Cipher
100.problem_statement: Write a Python function called encode_caesar() that takes in two parameters: plaintext (a string to be encrypted) and key (an integer shift value, which can be positive or negative). The function encrypts each alphabetic character by shifting it by key positions in the alphabet, wrapping around if necessary. Non-alphabetic characters are left unchanged. Case is preserved. Return the encrypted ciphertext. For example, encode_caesar('abc', 1) returns 'bcd', and encode_caesar('xyz', 3) returns 'abc'. encode_caesar('Hello!', 0) returns 'Hello!'.

101.title: Find Longest Consecutive Sequence
keywords: list traversing and computation of each element in nested loops
101.problem_statement: Write a Python function called find_longest_consecutive(). The function takes an unsorted list of integers nums and returns the length of the longest sequence of consecutive integers that appears in the list. For example, find_longest_consecutive([100, 4, 200, 1, 3, 2]) returns 4 (the sequence 1, 2, 3, 4). If the list is empty, return 0. You should NOT use Python's built-in sort() or sorted() functions.

102.title: Run-Length Encoding
102.problem_statement: Write a Python function called run_length_encode(). The function takes a string s and returns a run-length encoded version of it. In run-length encoding, consecutive repeated characters are replaced by the character followed by the count of repetitions. If a character appears only once, just include the character without a count. For example, run_length_encode('aabbbcccc') returns 'a2b3c4', and run_length_encode('abcd') returns 'abcd'. If the string is empty, return an empty string.

103.title: Matrix Multiplication
keywords: list traversing and computation of each element in nested loops
103.problem_statement: Write a Python function called matrix_multiply(). The function takes two 2D lists of integers, matrix_a and matrix_b, and returns their matrix product as a 2D list. If the dimensions are incompatible (number of columns of matrix_a does not equal number of rows of matrix_b), return an empty list. For example, matrix_multiply([[1,2],[3,4]], [[5,6],[7,8]]) returns [[19,22],[43,50]].

104.title: Longest Common Subsequence Length
keywords: comparing indexes in lists, strings, or tuples
104.problem_statement: Write a Python function called lcs_length(). The function takes two strings s1 and s2 and returns the length of their Longest Common Subsequence (LCS). A subsequence is a sequence derived from another sequence by deleting some elements without changing the order of the remaining elements. For example, lcs_length('abcde', 'ace') returns 3 (the LCS is 'ace'). lcs_length('abc', 'abc') returns 3. lcs_length('abc', 'def') returns 0.

105.title: Decode Run-Length String
105.problem_statement: Write a Python function called run_length_decode(). The function takes a run-length encoded string s and returns the decoded (original) string. In the encoded string, each character may be followed by a number indicating repetitions. If no number follows, the character appears once. For example, run_length_decode('a2b3c4') returns 'aabbbcccc', and run_length_decode('abcd') returns 'abcd'. If the string is empty, return an empty string.
105.solution:
def run_length_decode(s):
    if not s:
        return ''
    result = ''
    i = 0
    while i < len(s):
        ch = s[i]
        i += 1
        num_str = ''
        while i < len(s) and s[i].isdigit():
            num_str += s[i]
            i += 1
        count = int(num_str) if num_str else 1
        result += ch * count
    return result

106.title: Anagram Groups
106.problem_statement: Write a Python function called group_anagrams(). The function takes a list of strings words and returns a list of groups, where each group is a list of words that are anagrams of each other. Words are anagrams if they contain the same characters the same number of times. The order of groups and order within each group does not matter. For example, group_anagrams(['eat', 'tea', 'tan', 'ate', 'nat', 'bat']) returns [['eat','tea','ate'],['tan','nat'],['bat']] (in any order).
test_cases: assert sorted([sorted(g) for g in group_anagrams(['ab','ba'])]) == [['ab','ba']]

107.title: Spiral Matrix Traversal
107.problem_statement: Write a Python function called spiral_order(). The function takes a 2D list (matrix) of integers and returns a list of all elements in spiral order (clockwise, starting from the top-left). For example, spiral_order([[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]]) returns [1,2,3,6,9,8,7,4,5]. If the matrix is empty, return an empty list.

108.title: Food Delivery Cost Optimizer
108.problem_statement: Write a Python function called find_cheapest_bundle(). The function takes a list of tuples items (each tuple is (item_name, price)) and an integer budget. Find all possible combinations of items whose total price does not exceed the budget and return the combination that maximizes the total price. Return the list of item names in that combination. If no items can be bought, return an empty list. If multiple combinations share the same maximum total, return any one of them.

109.title: Housing Allocation Score
109.problem_statement: Write a Python function called rank_housing_applicants(). The function takes a list of dictionaries applicants, where each dictionary has keys 'name' (string), 'income' (int), 'family_size' (int), and 'years_waiting' (int). Compute a priority score for each applicant as: score = (family_size * 10) + (years_waiting * 5) - (income // 1000). Return a list of applicant names sorted by score in descending order (highest priority first). For equal scores, sort alphabetically by name.
109.solution:
def rank_housing_applicants(applicants):
    scored = []
    for a in applicants:
        score = (a['family_size'] * 10) + (a['years_waiting'] * 5) - (a['income'] // 1000)
        scored.append((score, a['name']))
    scored.sort(key=lambda x: (-x[0], x[1]))
    return [name for score, name in scored]

110.title: Online Shopping Cart Discount
110.problem_statement: Write a Python function called apply_discounts(). The function takes a list of dictionaries cart (each dict has 'name', 'price', 'quantity') and a dictionary discount_rules (maps category to discount percentage as a float between 0 and 1). Each item also has a 'category' key. Apply the discount for each item's category (if the category is in discount_rules), then compute the total cost. Return a tuple (itemized_costs, total) where itemized_costs is a dictionary mapping item name to its discounted subtotal (price * quantity * (1 - discount)), and total is the sum. Round each subtotal to 2 decimal places.

111.title: Swimming Training Schedule
111.problem_statement: Write a Python function called plan_training_sessions(). The function takes a dictionary swimmers where each key is a swimmer's name and the value is a list of their available time slots (strings like '08:00', '10:00', '14:00'). Find all time slots where at least min_swimmers swimmers are available. Return a dictionary mapping each qualifying time slot to the list of swimmer names available at that time. The parameter min_swimmers is also passed in.

112.title: All Older Than
112.problem_statement: Write a Python function called all_older_than(). The function takes two parameters: (1) A list of integers called age_list, where each element indicates the age of a person. (2) An integer called n, which is a threshold. The function returns True if ALL the age values in age_list are larger than n, and False otherwise. For example, all_older_than([24, 36, 45, 21], 20) returns True, and all_older_than([24, 36, 45, 21], 23) returns False. If age_list is empty, the function returns True.

113.title: Count Vowels
113.problem_statement: Write a Python function called count_vowels(). The function takes a single string input_str and returns the number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u) in it. The function should ignore the case of the letters, meaning that both uppercase and lowercase vowels should be counted. For example, count_vowels('Hello World') returns 3.

114.title: Contains Duplicate
114.problem_statement: Write a Python function called contains_duplicate(). Given an integer list nums, return True if any value appears at least twice in the list, and return False if every element is distinct. For example, contains_duplicate([1, 2, 3, 1]) returns True, and contains_duplicate([1, 2, 3, 4]) returns False.

115.title: Is Uppercase String
115.problem_statement: Write a Python function called is_uppercase_string(). The function takes a single string s and returns True if ALL alphabetic characters in the string are uppercase, and False otherwise. Non-alphabetic characters (spaces, digits, punctuation) are ignored. If the string has no alphabetic characters, return True. For example, is_uppercase_string('HELLO') returns True, and is_uppercase_string('Hello') returns False.

116.title: List Sum Greater Than Target
116.problem_statement: Write a Python function called sum_greater_than(). The function takes a list of integers nums and an integer target. The function returns True if the sum of all numbers in nums is greater than target, and False otherwise. For example, sum_greater_than([1, 2, 3], 5) returns True, and sum_greater_than([1, 2, 3], 6) returns False.
